_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from McLeod and Cooper JAMA 2012 (PMID 22474203). ATA guidelines 2014 on hypothyroidism. Clinical classification per Pearce et al. Eur Thyroid J 2019 (PMID 31768339). Postpartum and silent thyroiditis per Stagnaro-Green et al. JCEM 2011.

h1Title: Autoimmune Thyroiditis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Autoimmune thyroiditis is a chronic autoimmune inflammation of the thyroid gland driven by lymphocytic infiltration and autoantibodies against thyroid peroxidase (TPO) and thyroglobulin. Hashimoto thyroiditis — the goitrous form — is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient countries and affects approximately 1-2% of the general population, with a female-to-male ratio of 7:1. The condition progresses slowly over years from euthyroidism through subclinical to overt hypothyroidism in most patients, although a minority experience transient thyrotoxicosis early in the disease. Levothyroxine replacement is highly effective once overt hypothyroidism is established, restoring normal TSH in the vast majority of treated patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Positive TPO antibodies in 10-12% of the general adult population; overt hypothyroidism from autoimmune thyroiditis affects approximately 1-2% (Hollowell et al. JCEM 2002)
keyStats.demographics: Women 7-10 times more affected than men; peak diagnosis in the third to fifth decade; postpartum thyroiditis affects 5-10% of pregnancies
keyStats.avgAge: Hashimoto thyroiditis typically presents between ages 30-50; autoimmune thyroiditis can occur at any age including childhood
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 200-300 million people worldwide have thyroid autoantibodies; autoimmune thyroid disease is the leading cause of thyroid dysfunction globally

definition: Autoimmune thyroiditis (ICD-10: E06.3) is a spectrum of immune-mediated thyroid diseases characterized by lymphocytic infiltration of the thyroid parenchyma, formation of germinal centers, and autoantibody production against thyroid-specific antigens — most commonly thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) and thyroglobulin (anti-Tg). Hashimoto thyroiditis (chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis) is the classic goitrous form: diffuse goiter, progressive destruction of follicular cells, and eventual hypothyroidism over years to decades. Atrophic thyroiditis is the non-goitrous variant in which fibrosis and cell loss predominate, leading more rapidly to hypothyroidism and a small or impalpable gland. Silent (painless) thyroiditis and postpartum thyroiditis are transient autoimmune forms that cause a brief destructive thyrotoxicosis followed by hypothyroidism, then usually spontaneous recovery. Autoimmune thyroiditis sits within the broader category of organ-specific autoimmune diseases and shares genetic risk factors with type 1 diabetes, vitiligo, rheumatoid arthritis, and other autoimmune polyglandular syndromes. Endocrinology manages long-term care; primary care physicians typically monitor TSH and initiate levothyroxine replacement.

typesClassification.description: The most common form. A firm, diffuse or nodular goiter develops as lymphocytic infiltration expands the gland. TSH rises progressively as follicular cells are destroyed. Anti-TPO antibodies are positive in more than 90% of cases. Onset is insidious over years; overt hypothyroidism eventually develops in 30-50% of antibody-positive individuals.

Variant of autoimmune thyroiditis without goiter. Dominant anti-TSH-receptor blocking antibodies inhibit TSH stimulation, causing glandular atrophy and early hypothyroidism. Fibrosis is prominent on histology. The thyroid is small or impalpable on examination and imaging.

Transient autoimmune destructive thyroiditis causing a 2-4 month phase of thyrotoxicosis (from follicular cell lysis releasing stored hormone), followed by hypothyroidism lasting 4-8 weeks, then spontaneous recovery in 80% of patients. No neck pain. Radioiodine uptake is suppressed during the thyrotoxic phase.

The most common form of transient autoimmune thyroiditis; occurs in 5-10% of pregnancies. Typically presents 1-6 months postpartum; follows the same thyrotoxic-then-hypothyroid sequence as silent thyroiditis. Risk of permanent hypothyroidism is approximately 25% at 10 years, particularly in women who are anti-TPO positive in the first trimester.

Autoimmune thyroiditis in children and adolescents; accounts for most cases of acquired hypothyroidism in childhood. Clinical presentation is similar to adult Hashimoto thyroiditis but growth and pubertal delay may be prominent. Spontaneous remission of antibodies occurs in 20-30% of adolescents.

primarySymptoms: Persistent fatigue and low energy disproportionate to sleep quality, reflecting reduced cellular metabolism from declining thyroid hormone levels.
Weight gain of 2-10 kg over months to years without change in diet, from reduced basal metabolic rate as T3/T4 levels fall.
Cold intolerance — feeling unusually cold even in warm environments — from diminished thermogenesis, often noted before overt hypothyroidism is diagnosed on laboratory testing.
Dry skin, brittle nails, and coarse hair with increased hair loss (telogen effluvium), caused by reduced keratinocyte turnover and sebaceous gland function under hypothyroid conditions.
Constipation from reduced gut motility that worsens with progressive thyroid hormone deficiency.
Diffuse, rubbery, non-tender goiter in Hashimoto thyroiditis — often the finding that prompts laboratory work-up, though the gland may be normal-sized early in disease.
Cognitive slowing, difficulty concentrating, and memory complaints; patients commonly describe mental fog that improves with adequate levothyroxine replacement.
Menstrual irregularities in women — commonly oligomenorrhea, menorrhagia, or anovulatory cycles — from hypothalamic-pituitary dysregulation caused by low thyroid hormone.
Muscle aches, stiffness, and cramps (hypothyroid myopathy), occasionally with elevated creatine kinase, in patients with prolonged untreated hypothyroidism.

Transient palpitations, tremor, anxiety, and heat intolerance in the early thyrotoxic phase of silent or postpartum thyroiditis, often misattributed to anxiety disorders before thyroid function tests are checked.
earlyWarningSigns: Serum TSH mildly elevated above the upper reference limit (typically above 4.0-4.5 mIU/L) on routine blood testing, with normal free T4 — subclinical hypothyroidism that may progress to overt disease.
Positive anti-TPO antibodies on screening in a woman planning pregnancy or with a first-degree relative with autoimmune thyroid disease.
New small, firm goiter noticed on physical examination or incidentally on neck imaging, warranting thyroid function tests and antibody screen.
Unexplained fatigue, weight change, or menstrual irregularity in a woman aged 20-50, prompting TSH screening that reveals early hypothyroidism.
Brief unexplained thyrotoxicosis (palpitations, weight loss, anxiety) 1-6 months after delivery, signaling postpartum thyroiditis before the hypothyroid phase develops.
emergencySigns: Myxedema coma — altered consciousness, hypothermia, bradycardia, and hypoventilation in a severely hypothyroid patient — requires intensive care admission and IV T4 or T3 administration.
Thyroid storm during the thyrotoxic phase of destructive thyroiditis (rare), with heart rate above 140 bpm, hyperthermia, and agitation, requiring beta-blockers and supportive care.
Rapidly enlarging thyroid mass in known Hashimoto thyroiditis — rare risk of thyroid lymphoma developing on the background of chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis, requiring urgent biopsy.
Severe depression or psychosis in profound untreated hypothyroidism, sometimes called myxedema madness, requiring psychiatric support alongside thyroid replacement.

causes.cause: Autoimmune attack on thyroid peroxidase and thyroglobulin
causes.description: CD4+ T-helper cells recognize thyroid-specific antigens and drive both cytotoxic T-cell (cell-mediated) and antibody-mediated (anti-TPO, anti-Tg, anti-TSH-receptor blocking) destruction of thyroid follicular cells. The initiating antigen trigger remains uncertain but likely involves molecular mimicry with viral or bacterial antigens in genetically predisposed individuals.

HLA-DR3, HLA-DR4, and HLA-DR5 alleles confer two- to fourfold increased risk. Non-HLA susceptibility genes include CTLA-4, PTPN22, thyroglobulin gene variants, and CD25 (IL-2 receptor), all of which influence T-regulatory cell function and immune tolerance to self-antigens. First-degree relatives of affected individuals have a 2-5 times higher risk.

Excess dietary iodine can induce or accelerate autoimmune thyroiditis in genetically susceptible individuals. Areas of iodine supplementation programs have seen transient rises in thyroid autoantibody prevalence. Highly iodinated contrast media and amiodarone (37% iodine by weight) can trigger thyroiditis in predisposed patients.

Pregnancy induces immune tolerance that suppresses autoimmune activity; the postpartum immune rebound (3-12 months after delivery) triggers destructive thyroiditis in 5-10% of anti-TPO-positive women. Risk is highest in women who are anti-TPO positive in the first trimester or who have had a prior episode.

Selenium is required for the selenoprotein glutathione peroxidase that protects thyrocytes from hydrogen peroxide generated during thyroid hormone synthesis. Selenium deficiency — common in parts of Europe, sub-Saharan Africa, and China — is associated with higher anti-TPO titers and accelerated progression to hypothyroidism.

Anti-PD-1, anti-PD-L1, and anti-CTLA-4 immunotherapy agents used in cancer treatment cause autoimmune thyroiditis in 5-20% of patients, often presenting as transient thyrotoxicosis then permanent hypothyroidism. Thyroid function should be monitored every 4-6 weeks during checkpoint inhibitor treatment.

riskFactors.description: Women are 7-10 times more likely to develop autoimmune thyroiditis than men, attributed to sex-hormone effects on immune regulation and X-chromosome linkage of immune-related genes.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of autoimmune thyroid disease
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of individuals with Hashimoto thyroiditis or Graves disease have a 2-5 times higher risk. Concordance in identical twins is approximately 55%, indicating both strong genetic and environmental contributions.

Type 1 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, celiac disease, Addison disease, and vitiligo each confer additional thyroid autoimmunity risk. Autoimmune polyglandular syndromes include thyroiditis as a core component.
Postpartum thyroiditis occurs in 5-10% of pregnancies; women with anti-TPO antibodies in the first trimester have a 30-50% risk of postpartum thyroiditis.

Supplementation above recommended daily intake (adults 150 µg/day), high-iodine diets (seaweed, high-dose iodine supplements), or drugs with high iodine content (amiodarone, radiocontrast) can trigger or worsen autoimmune thyroiditis in predisposed individuals.

Selenium intake below 30-40 µg/day is associated with higher autoantibody titers. Selenium supplementation (200 µg/day) has reduced TPO antibody levels in small trials, though benefit for disease progression is not yet established.
Up to 20% of patients treated with anti-PD-1 or anti-CTLA-4 agents develop thyroid dysfunction, predominantly autoimmune thyroiditis leading to hypothyroidism.

affectedDemographics: Women of reproductive and perimenopausal age (30-50 years) carry the highest burden; anti-TPO antibody prevalence in women exceeds 15% in population surveys.
Children and adolescents account for the majority of acquired hypothyroidism in developed countries through autoimmune thyroiditis; growth and pubertal delay are frequent presenting features.
Postpartum women: autoimmune thyroiditis is the most common thyroid disorder in the first year after delivery, affecting up to 10% of pregnancies worldwide.
Patients with Down syndrome, Turner syndrome, or Klinefelter syndrome have markedly elevated rates of autoimmune thyroiditis — up to 30% in Down syndrome cohorts.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis rests on two independent pillars: biochemical evidence of thyroid dysfunction and evidence of autoimmune etiology. The initial test is serum TSH — elevated TSH (above 4.0-4.5 mIU/L) with low or normal free T4 defines the stage of hypothyroidism. Anti-TPO antibodies are measured to confirm the autoimmune cause; they are positive in over 90% of Hashimoto thyroiditis and 70-80% of atrophic thyroiditis. Anti-thyroglobulin antibodies are measured when anti-TPO is negative and autoimmune disease is still suspected. In the transient thyrotoxic phase of silent or postpartum thyroiditis, TSH is suppressed, free T4 or T3 may be mildly elevated, and radioiodine uptake is characteristically low or absent (distinguishing it from Graves disease where uptake is elevated). Thyroid ultrasound is not required for diagnosis but is indicated when a nodule or heterogeneous echo pattern is palpated — classic findings are diffuse hypoechogenicity and pseudonodular heterogeneity with increased vascularity. Fine-needle aspiration biopsy (FNAC) of focal thyroid nodules is performed when ultrasound features suggest malignancy. TSH-receptor antibodies (TRAb) are measured when Graves disease is in the differential. American Thyroid Association (ATA) 2014 guidelines recommend treatment when TSH exceeds 10 mIU/L with symptoms, or when TSH is above 4.5 mIU/L with symptoms or pregnancy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line test; elevated TSH indicates hypothyroidism, the most common functional consequence of autoimmune thyroiditis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; most labs report within 4-8 hours. TSH above 4.0-4.5 mIU/L on two separate occasions confirms hypothyroidism. During the thyrotoxic phase of silent thyroiditis, TSH is suppressed below 0.1 mIU/L.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the unbound, biologically active hormone; low free T4 with elevated TSH confirms overt hypothyroidism
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Drawn at the same time as TSH. Results in 4-8 hours. Free T4 within the reference range with elevated TSH is subclinical hypothyroidism; below range is overt hypothyroidism.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the autoimmune etiology; positive in over 90% of Hashimoto thyroiditis cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 1-3 days from most reference labs. Very high titers (above 100 IU/mL) are strongly associated with autoimmune thyroiditis, though the titer does not predict disease severity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Positive in up to 60% of Hashimoto cases; tested when anti-TPO is negative and autoimmune thyroiditis is still suspected
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw alongside anti-TPO. Results within 1-3 days. Anti-Tg is less specific than anti-TPO but adds diagnostic sensitivity when both tests are used together.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes gland size, echogenicity, nodularity, and vascularity; distinguishes Hashimoto from multinodular goiter or other causes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless 15-20 minute scan. Classic Hashimoto findings are diffuse heterogeneous hypoechogenicity. Identifies nodules requiring FNAC and rare thyroid lymphoma appearing as rapidly growing hypoechoic mass.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes low-uptake destructive thyroiditis (silent, postpartum) from Graves disease (high uptake) in the thyrotoxic phase
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A tracer dose of radioiodine or technetium-99m pertechnetate is given orally or IV; uptake is measured at 2-24 hours. Not used in pregnancy. Results within 24 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of autoimmune thyroiditis is directed at the functional consequence — hypothyroidism — rather than the autoimmune process itself, as no intervention has yet been shown to halt thyroid destruction reliably. Levothyroxine (LT4) is the standard of care for overt hypothyroidism (TSH above 10 mIU/L or TSH above 4.5 mIU/L with symptoms) per ATA 2014 and European Thyroid Association 2013 guidelines. The starting dose is typically 1.5-1.8 µg/kg/day in healthy adults, with dose adjustment based on TSH every 6-8 weeks until the target TSH of 0.5-2.5 mIU/L is achieved. In elderly patients and those with cardiovascular disease, a lower starting dose (25-50 µg/day) with gradual titration is recommended to avoid cardiac arrhythmia. Levothyroxine should be taken fasting, 30-60 minutes before food or other medications, to maximize absorption. Combined levothyroxine plus liothyronine (T3) therapy has been studied in randomized trials but has not consistently shown benefit over LT4 monotherapy and is not currently recommended as first-line in most guidelines. For subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 4.5-10 mIU/L), levothyroxine is recommended in pregnancy and when TSH exceeds 10 mIU/L; for TSH 4.5-10 mIU/L without symptoms or pregnancy, treatment decisions are individualized. Selenium supplementation at 200 µg/day for 12 months reduces anti-TPO titers in some randomized trials, but no guideline yet mandates it outside of selenium-deficient regions. The transient thyrotoxic phase of silent and postpartum thyroiditis requires only symptomatic management with a beta-blocker (propranolol 20-40 mg twice daily) and monitoring; antithyroid drugs are not effective because hormone is released from stored pools, not newly synthesized.

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine (LT4) 1.5-1.8 µg/kg/day orally
medicalTreatments.description: First-line replacement for overt hypothyroidism. Taken fasting 30-60 minutes before breakfast; absorption impaired by calcium, iron, proton pump inhibitors, and high-fiber foods. Dose adjusted every 6-8 weeks based on TSH until target TSH 0.5-2.5 mIU/L is achieved.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalizes TSH in more than 95% of patients; most symptom resolution within 6-12 weeks of reaching euthyroid range

medicalTreatments.name: Selenium supplementation 200 µg/day (selenomethionine)
medicalTreatments.description: Supplementation for 6-12 months reduces anti-TPO antibody titers in randomized controlled trials, particularly in selenium-deficient patients. European Thyroid Association 2021 recommends consideration in anti-TPO-positive women planning pregnancy in selenium-deficient regions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces anti-TPO titers by 30-50% in RCT meta-analyses; effect on progression to hypothyroidism is not yet proven

medicalTreatments.name: Propranolol 20-40 mg twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: Beta-blockade for symptomatic relief of palpitations, tremor, and heat intolerance during the thyrotoxic phase of silent or postpartum thyroiditis. Dose tapered as thyrotoxicosis resolves (typically 6-12 weeks).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control within 24-48 hours; does not alter disease course or duration of thyrotoxic phase

medicalTreatments.name: Liothyronine (T3) added to levothyroxine (combination therapy)
medicalTreatments.description: A subset of patients with persistent symptoms on LT4 monotherapy may benefit from combined T4/T3. Typical approach adds 5-10 µg T3 while reducing LT4 by 25-50 µg to maintain euthyroid TSH. Not endorsed as first-line by ATA or ETA but is a reasonable second-line option in selected patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No consistent benefit over LT4 monotherapy in individual trials; a minority of patients report subjective improvement in cognition and wellbeing

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous levothyroxine (myxedema coma)
medicalTreatments.description: IV LT4 200-400 µg loading dose followed by 50-100 µg/day, combined with IV hydrocortisone 100 mg every 8 hours (to cover possible concurrent adrenal insufficiency) and supportive ICU care. Used when oral administration is impossible.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Survival rate above 60-70% with prompt treatment in specialized centers; mortality rises to above 40-50% with delayed treatment

surgicalOptions.name: Thyroidectomy (total or near-total)
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for compressive goiter causing dysphagia or dyspnea, cosmetically disfiguring goiter, or suspected thyroid lymphoma. After thyroidectomy, permanent levothyroxine replacement is required.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom relief from compression in over 90% of patients; complication rate (hypoparathyroidism, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury) approximately 1-2% in high-volume centers

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN)
alternativeTreatments.description: Studied at doses of 1.5-4.5 mg/day in small open-label trials for autoimmune conditions including autoimmune thyroiditis; proposed mechanism is modulation of T-regulatory cells. Evidence base is too limited for routine recommendation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Gluten-free diet in celiac disease co-occurrence
alternativeTreatments.description: Celiac disease is overrepresented in autoimmune thyroiditis cohorts. A strict gluten-free diet reduces anti-TPO titers in celiac patients with autoimmune thyroiditis in observational studies, likely through resolution of gut mucosal inflammation.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist should evaluate any patient with a new goiter, positive thyroid autoantibodies, subclinical hypothyroidism in pregnancy, or persistent symptoms despite levothyroxine therapy at apparently adequate TSH levels. Specialist input is also warranted when distinguishing postpartum thyroiditis from Graves disease, and when silent thyroiditis is suspected.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an endocrinologist or thyroid specialist experienced in managing autoimmune thyroid disease. For women planning pregnancy or currently pregnant with positive anti-TPO antibodies, an endocrinologist with expertise in thyroid disease in pregnancy is essential. Most stable, well-controlled patients are managed in primary care with periodic TSH monitoring.
hospitalCriteria: Thyroid ultrasound and fine-needle aspiration biopsy service for nodule assessment
Radioiodine uptake scanning capability to differentiate destructive thyroiditis from Graves disease
Thyroid function test laboratory with rapid turnaround
Endocrinology outpatient and inpatient consultation services
Surgical thyroidectomy service for compressive or malignant goiter
keyFacilities: Endocrinology outpatient clinics with thyroid-function monitoring
Obstetric endocrinology services for thyroid disease in pregnancy
preventionStrategies: Maintain selenium-adequate diet (Brazil nuts, fish, meat) particularly in selenium-deficient geographic regions where supplementation may reduce autoantibody burden.
Avoid iodine supplementation above recommended daily intake (150 µg/day for adults) without medical supervision, especially when there is a family history of autoimmune thyroid disease.
Women who are anti-TPO positive should have TSH measured before and during pregnancy to enable early treatment and prevent obstetric complications.
Patients receiving immune checkpoint inhibitor cancer therapy should have baseline and serial thyroid function tests every 4-6 weeks to detect thyroiditis early.
First-degree relatives of Hashimoto thyroiditis patients should be screened with TSH and anti-TPO antibodies, particularly women aged 30 and over or those planning pregnancy.
lifestyleModifications: Take levothyroxine at the same time each morning, fasting, 30-60 minutes before food and away from calcium, iron, and magnesium supplements.
Maintain consistent iodine intake from a balanced diet; avoid very high iodine supplements and excessive seaweed consumption.
Regular moderate aerobic exercise (30 minutes daily) improves fatigue, mood, and cardiovascular risk, all of which are elevated in hypothyroidism.
Ensure adequate vitamin D levels, as deficiency is more prevalent in autoimmune thyroiditis and may modulate immune activity.
Avoid raw cruciferous vegetables (kale, broccoli) in very large amounts, as isothiocyanates can mildly inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis — though normal dietary quantities are safe.
Monitor and report new neck swelling, dysphagia, or rapid goiter growth to exclude thyroid lymphoma or nodular transformation.

recommended: Selenium-rich foods (Brazil nuts, tuna, sardines, sunflower seeds) to support thyrocyte protection and potentially moderate autoantibody levels
Adequate iodine from dietary sources (dairy, seafood, iodized salt) within recommended daily intake
Vitamin D-rich foods and sunlight exposure given the high prevalence of vitamin D deficiency in autoimmune thyroiditis cohorts
Iron-rich foods after confirming iron status, since hypothyroidism reduces gut iron absorption
avoid: High-dose iodine supplements (above 500 µg/day) or daily large quantities of seaweed (kombu, kelp), which can acutely worsen autoimmune thyroiditis
Raw cruciferous vegetables in very large therapeutic quantities (kilograms per day) that could inhibit thyroid peroxidase activity
Soy isoflavone supplements taken within 4 hours of levothyroxine, as they impair tablet absorption
Calcium and iron supplements taken simultaneously with levothyroxine, reducing absorption by 20-40%

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise for 30 minutes five days per week is recommended for patients with autoimmune thyroiditis. Hypothyroidism causes muscle weakness and reduced cardiac output; exercise tolerance returns with optimal levothyroxine replacement. Patients with uncontrolled severe hypothyroidism should defer vigorous exercise until TSH is normalized, as the risk of exertional rhabdomyolysis and cardiac arrhythmia is elevated.
dailyManagement: Take levothyroxine every morning at the same time, on an empty stomach, at least 30 minutes before food or other medications.
Check TSH every 6-8 weeks after any dose change; once stable, annual monitoring is sufficient in most patients.
Report new or worsening symptoms — persistent fatigue, weight regain, constipation, or mood changes — as they may indicate under-replacement that requires dose adjustment.
Keep a list of any medications or supplements started or stopped, as many interact with levothyroxine absorption.
Inform any new prescribing clinician about levothyroxine therapy, as several drugs (rifampicin, phenytoin, cholestyramine) accelerate T4 metabolism or impair absorption.
Women should notify their endocrinologist as soon as pregnancy is confirmed or planned, as levothyroxine requirements increase by 25-50% from the first trimester.
prognosis: Autoimmune thyroiditis is a lifelong condition but, with levothyroxine replacement, quality of life is normal in the majority of patients. Overt hypothyroidism develops in approximately 4% per year among women with positive anti-TPO antibodies and normal baseline TSH; over 10-20 years, roughly 30-50% progress to overt hypothyroidism requiring treatment. Patients treated to a TSH target of 0.5-2.5 mIU/L have cardiovascular, metabolic, and fertility outcomes comparable to the general population. Postpartum thyroiditis resolves spontaneously in approximately 75% of women, but the remaining 25% develop permanent hypothyroidism by 10 years. A small excess risk of thyroid lymphoma exists with longstanding autoimmune thyroiditis — incidence is approximately 60-fold above the general population in absolute terms, but thyroid lymphoma remains rare overall (3 per million per year in the general population). Thyroid carcinoma risk is not significantly elevated by autoimmune thyroiditis per se, though coexistence is common.
recoveryTimeline: In overt hypothyroidism, symptom improvement begins within 2-4 weeks of starting levothyroxine and most patients reach target TSH within 6-12 weeks. Full subjective recovery typically takes 3-6 months. In postpartum thyroiditis, the thyrotoxic phase resolves in 4-12 weeks, followed by a hypothyroid phase lasting 4-8 weeks; most women are euthyroid by 12 months. Hashimoto thyroiditis does not remit but is controlled indefinitely with levothyroxine replacement.
complications: Overt hypothyroidism with cardiovascular consequences — elevated LDL cholesterol, impaired cardiac contractility, and pericardial effusion — if untreated for months to years.
Infertility, recurrent miscarriage, and gestational hypothyroidism causing impaired fetal neurodevelopment when thyroid disease is unrecognized or undertreated in pregnancy.
Myxedema coma — a rare but life-threatening complication of profound hypothyroidism triggered by cold exposure, infection, or sedative drugs, with 20-40% mortality even in treated patients.
Thyroid lymphoma arising in the background of chronic Hashimoto thyroiditis — presents as rapidly enlarging, firm neck mass requiring urgent biopsy.
Coexisting autoimmune conditions (type 1 diabetes, Addison disease, celiac disease) that may develop years after autoimmune thyroiditis is diagnosed.

Fracture risk from over-replacement with levothyroxine causing subclinical or overt thyrotoxicosis, particularly in postmenopausal women — TSH should not be suppressed below 0.5 mIU/L on replacement therapy.

supportResources.name: American Thyroid Association — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient guides on hypothyroidism, Hashimoto thyroiditis, and thyroid disease in pregnancy from the leading North American thyroid specialty society.

supportResources.name: British Thyroid Foundation
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing information on Hashimoto thyroiditis, levothyroxine management, and research updates.

supportResources.name: Thyroid UK
supportResources.description: Patient-run UK organization offering peer support, treatment guides, and a forum for patients with autoimmune thyroiditis and hypothyroidism.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Autoimmune thyroiditis in its early or transient phase can cause brief thyrotoxicosis with suppressed TSH, mimicking hyperthyroidism. However, in destructive thyroiditis radioiodine uptake is low or absent (released hormone, not new synthesis), and antithyroid drugs are ineffective. Graves disease — the most common cause of true hyperthyroidism — has elevated radioiodine uptake and TSH-receptor stimulating antibodies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute thyroiditis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute (suppurative) thyroiditis is a bacterial infection causing severe anterior neck pain, fever, and systemic toxicity. Autoimmune thyroiditis is painless (Hashimoto) or only mildly tender (silent), non-infectious, and causes thyroid dysfunction rather than neck infection. Blood cultures and neck imaging distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Subacute thyroiditis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis is a post-viral inflammatory condition causing severe neck pain radiating to the ear, markedly elevated ESR, and transient thyrotoxicosis. Autoimmune thyroiditis is painless and associated with anti-TPO antibodies; subacute thyroiditis is seronegative and self-limited over 2-6 months.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune thyroiditis is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient countries; the two conditions are inseparable in clinical practice.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Graves disease — the most common cause of hyperthyroidism — shares autoimmune etiology and can coexist with or evolve from autoimmune thyroiditis in the same patient.

relatedConditions.name: Hashimoto disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Hashimoto thyroiditis is the most common form of autoimmune thyroiditis; the two terms are often used interchangeably in clinical practice.

relatedConditions.name: Thyroiditis
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune thyroiditis is one of several types of thyroiditis; understanding the spectrum aids differential diagnosis and management.

relatedConditions.name: Pituitary disorders
relatedConditions.relevance: Secondary hypothyroidism from pituitary failure must be distinguished from autoimmune thyroiditis; both cause low thyroid hormone but TSH is elevated in autoimmune disease and low or normal in pituitary disease.

faqs.question: What is autoimmune thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Autoimmune thyroiditis is chronic inflammation of the thyroid gland caused by the immune system attacking thyroid cells. The most common form is Hashimoto thyroiditis. It is the leading cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient countries, affecting roughly 1-2% of adults, predominantly women.

faqs.question: Is autoimmune thyroiditis the same as Hashimoto disease?
faqs.answer: Hashimoto thyroiditis is the most common form of autoimmune thyroiditis — the goitrous, chronic variant. Other forms include atrophic thyroiditis, silent thyroiditis, and postpartum thyroiditis. All share lymphocytic infiltration and anti-TPO antibodies but differ in gland size and clinical course.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of autoimmune thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: The most common symptoms reflect hypothyroidism: fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, dry skin, constipation, and cognitive slowing. A firm goiter may be palpable in Hashimoto thyroiditis. Some patients experience brief palpitations and heat intolerance during the early thyrotoxic phase before hypothyroidism develops.

faqs.question: How is autoimmune thyroiditis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires an elevated TSH (above 4.0-4.5 mIU/L), reduced free T4 confirming hypothyroidism, and positive anti-TPO antibodies confirming the autoimmune cause. Thyroid ultrasound is not required for diagnosis but shows diffuse hypoechogenicity in Hashimoto thyroiditis.

faqs.question: Does autoimmune thyroiditis always need treatment?
faqs.answer: Treatment is recommended when TSH exceeds 10 mIU/L, when TSH is above 4.5 mIU/L with symptoms, or in pregnancy. Subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 4.5-10 mIU/L) without symptoms in non-pregnant adults is monitored rather than automatically treated. Anti-TPO positivity without hypothyroidism requires monitoring only.

faqs.question: What medication is used to treat autoimmune thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Levothyroxine (synthetic T4) is the standard treatment, taken daily on an empty stomach. The dose is adjusted every 6-8 weeks until TSH reaches 0.5-2.5 mIU/L. Selenium supplementation (200 µg/day) may reduce antibody titers but is not standard first-line therapy.

faqs.question: Can autoimmune thyroiditis be cured?
faqs.answer: There is currently no treatment that stops the autoimmune process and cures autoimmune thyroiditis. Levothyroxine replacement fully controls hypothyroidism in most patients. Postpartum and silent thyroiditis resolve spontaneously in about 75% of cases. For Hashimoto thyroiditis, lifelong monitoring and, when hypothyroid, lifelong replacement are the standard.

faqs.question: Is autoimmune thyroiditis hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a strong genetic component. First-degree relatives of affected individuals have a 2-5 times higher risk. HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR5 alleles and genes including CTLA-4 and PTPN22 are implicated. Both genes and environmental factors — iodine, selenium, infection — contribute to disease onset.

faqs.question: Can autoimmune thyroiditis affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated hypothyroidism in pregnancy increases risks of miscarriage, preterm birth, and impaired fetal neurodevelopment. Women with positive anti-TPO antibodies should have TSH checked before conception and in each trimester; levothyroxine requirements increase by about 25-50% in the first trimester.

faqs.question: What is postpartum thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Postpartum thyroiditis is an autoimmune thyroiditis that develops 1-6 months after delivery in 5-10% of women. It typically causes brief thyrotoxicosis, then hypothyroidism, then recovery over 12 months. About 25% of affected women develop permanent hypothyroidism requiring lifelong levothyroxine.

faqs.question: Do anti-TPO antibodies always cause symptoms?
faqs.answer: No. Many people have positive anti-TPO antibodies with normal TSH and no symptoms; they are at increased risk of progression to hypothyroidism (about 4% per year in women) but do not need treatment. Annual TSH monitoring is recommended once antibodies are positive.

faqs.question: Can children get autoimmune thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Autoimmune thyroiditis is the most common cause of acquired hypothyroidism in children and adolescents. It can cause poor growth, delayed puberty, and learning difficulties. Down syndrome, Turner syndrome, and type 1 diabetes are associated with a much higher risk.

faqs.question: Does diet affect autoimmune thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Selenium-rich foods (Brazil nuts, tuna) may help reduce anti-TPO antibody levels. Excess iodine from supplements or seaweed can worsen autoimmune thyroiditis. Calcium, iron, and soy should not be taken within 4 hours of levothyroxine. Gluten-free diet may help in patients with confirmed coexisting celiac disease.

faqs.question: How often should TSH be checked in autoimmune thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Once on a stable levothyroxine dose, TSH is checked annually. During dose titration, TSH is re-checked 6-8 weeks after each adjustment. In pregnancy, TSH is checked at booking and in each trimester. Anti-TPO-positive patients with normal TSH should be tested annually.

faqs.question: Can autoimmune thyroiditis increase cancer risk?
faqs.answer: Thyroid lymphoma is approximately 60 times more common in autoimmune thyroiditis than the general population, but the absolute risk remains very low — roughly 3 per million per year. Thyroid carcinoma risk is not meaningfully elevated. Rapid goiter enlargement warrants urgent evaluation.

faqs.question: What is the difference between autoimmune thyroiditis and subacute thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis causes severe neck pain, fever, and markedly elevated ESR after a viral illness, and is self-limited. Autoimmune thyroiditis is painless, chronic, and antibody-mediated. Subacute thyroiditis is seronegative for anti-TPO antibodies in most cases and resolves without hormone replacement in the majority.

metaDescription: Autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto disease): leading cause of hypothyroidism. TPO antibodies, levothyroxine treatment, postpartum forms and prognosis reviewed.
